trust_assumption: A data lakehouse provider (Snowflake share / Databricks Delta Sharing / BigQuery Analytics Hub) treats a share grant as a bilateral, terminal trust edge - 'provider account P grants recipient account R read on object O' - and assumes the recipient is the consuming endpoint, so it never re-evaluates whether R has itself re-shared O onward, mounted it into a second metastore, or exposed it through an open Delta Sharing recipient token, letting the original provider's access decision silently propagate across a share graph the provider can no longer see.
violation: An attacker who controls or compromises a downstream recipient account (a partner workspace, an over-permissive analytics-hub listing, or a leaked Delta Sharing bearer token) re-exposes the shared dataset to accounts the provider never authorized, so a single grant fans out transitively across organizational boundaries; because each hop is a legitimate platform share operation logged under the recipient's identity, the provider's audit shows only the first authorized edge and never the onward propagation that turned a scoped partner grant into broad multi-tenant exposure.

techniques.name: Onward re-share of a received lakehouse dataset beyond provider authorization
Leaked Delta Sharing recipient token granting unscoped external read

rationale: Lakehouse data sharing (Snowflake Secure Data Sharing, Databricks Delta Sharing, BigQuery Analytics Hub) is the dominant 2025-2027 mechanism for cross-org data exchange, and its trust model is per-edge: the provider authorizes a recipient and reasons about that one bilateral grant, while the protocol (especially open Delta Sharing with long-lived recipient bearer tokens) and the recipient's own re-share rights let access propagate transitively to accounts the provider never evaluated. The unmodeled seam is identity-to-authority across a share graph the provider cannot observe: the provider's access decision is trusted to bound who can read the data, but onward re-share, secondary metastore mounts, and token leakage break that bound while every hop remains a sanctioned, separately-logged platform operation. This is distinct from the SaaS-OAuth transitive-scope gap (which composes scopes across first-party app integrations in one tenant) and from generic cloud bucket exposure because the trust object is specifically the lakehouse share-grant edge and its assumed terminality across organizational data-marketplace boundaries. There is no cross-provider standard that propagates or revokes share authority transitively, no provider-side visibility into recipient re-sharing, and detection of onward propagation is absent.
suggested_research: Build a share-graph mapper that, from a provider vantage, enumerates recipients and infers onward re-share and token exposure across Snowflake/Databricks/BigQuery sharing; demonstrate that a scoped partner grant reaches unauthorized accounts via recipient re-share or a leaked Delta Sharing token, then propose transitive grant provenance and revocation plus provider-visible re-share telemetry.

trust_assumption: A managed KMS (AWS KMS / GCP Cloud KMS / Azure Key Vault) treats a Decrypt grant as authority bound to the named principal and trusts that whoever holds that principal's identity is the intended caller, so it will unwrap any ciphertext blob presented to it under that key without checking which workload, request context, or data-object the decrypt is for - and because envelope encryption reuses one data-key-encrypting key across thousands of objects, a single granted Decrypt right is implicitly an oracle for the entire envelope-encrypted corpus.
violation: An attacker who obtains the grantee role (a compromised compute identity, an assumed cross-account role, or a confused-deputy service that decrypts on the caller's behalf) replays arbitrary ciphertext blobs from a stolen storage tier to the KMS and harvests plaintext at will, because KMS authorizes on principal identity rather than on the legitimacy of this specific decrypt; encryption-context conditions, where they exist, are unset or attacker-supplied, so one decrypt grant decrypts everything the envelope key ever wrapped rather than only the object the grant was intended for.

techniques.name: Ciphertext-replay decrypt oracle under a stolen KMS grantee identity
Confused-deputy KMS decrypt via service that unwraps on caller behalf

rationale: Cloud envelope encryption is near-universal for data-at-rest in 2025-2027, and KMS authorization is identity-centric: a Decrypt grant authorizes a principal, not a particular object or workload, so any holder of that principal can present any ciphertext wrapped by the same KEK and get plaintext, turning the KMS into a decryption oracle for the whole envelope corpus. The unmodeled seam is identity-to-authority decoupled from the data being decrypted: encryption-context binding exists but is widely unset or controlled by the caller, and confused-deputy services that decrypt on a caller's behalf launder the request, so the trust 'this grant unlocks the data this caller is entitled to' silently becomes 'this grant unlocks everything the key wrapped.' This is distinct from the secrets-lease-rotation-window gap (a timing race on lease validity) and from generic stolen-credential use because the trust object is the KMS decrypt grant's implicit scoping to a single intended decrypt, broken by ciphertext replay and KEK reuse. Detection that flags anomalous decrypt volume or ciphertext from outside the grantee's normal object set is nascent, and there is no enforced object-level binding on most envelope schemes.
suggested_research: Audit real envelope-encryption deployments for unset or attacker-controlled encryption-context and KEK reuse breadth; build a decrypt-oracle harness that, given a grantee role, recovers plaintext from replayed ciphertext across objects the grant never intended, and propose mandatory object-scoped encryption-context binding plus KMS-side anomaly detection on decrypt request provenance.

trust_assumption: A CI/CD platform trusts that a secret injected into a build is ephemeral and confined to the running job - present only as a masked environment variable for the step that needs it - so it treats the build cache, the produced container image layers, and the job log stream as non-sensitive build byproducts that can be persisted, shared across jobs, cached publicly, and retained indefinitely without secret-grade controls.
violation: A secret leaks out of its intended ephemeral scope into a durable artifact - baked into an intermediate container layer by a RUN that referenced it, written to a restored/cross-job build cache, or echoed into a job log that masking missed because the value was transformed, base64'd, or split - and an attacker who can read those persisted byproducts (a public layer, a shared cache key, a retained log) recovers a live credential long after the job ended, crossing from a transient build context into standing access the platform never modeled as a secret store.

techniques.name: Credential recovery from intermediate container image layers
Secret residue in shared/restored CI build cache or job logs

rationale: CI/CD secret injection assumes ephemerality, but the surrounding build infrastructure - layer caches, cross-job and remote build caches, and retained log streams - is engineered for durability and sharing, and 2025-2027 supply-chain incidents repeatedly show live credentials recovered from published image layers and cached build state. The unmodeled seam is provenance/lifetime: a value is trusted to remain a transient in-job secret, but the build byproducts that capture it are governed as non-sensitive plumbing, so masking (which only redacts exact verbatim matches in the log channel) and ephemeral scoping both fail against transformed values and persisted caches/layers. This is distinct from the context-window-secret-persistence gap (LLM transcript export) and from classic hardcoded-secret findings because the trust object is the assumed confinement of an injected secret to its job, broken specifically by the cache/layer/log durability of the build platform rather than by source-code exposure. Provenance-aware secret tracking across cache and layer boundaries, and detection of transformed-secret leakage that bypasses verbatim masking, are nascent.
suggested_research: Build a secret-flow tracer that follows an injected credential through build cache restoration, image layer construction, and log emission - detecting transformed/encoded leakage that verbatim masking misses - and quantify how often live secrets survive in public layers and shared caches; propose taint-tracked secret scoping and layer/cache redaction that treats all build byproducts as secret-bearing until proven clean.

trust_assumption: An event-streaming bus (Kafka / Pub/Sub / Kinesis / EventBridge) trusts that a workload granted consumer access to the cluster will read only the topics its application logic concerns, so authorization is configured coarsely - wildcard topic ACLs, a shared service principal across microservices, or default 'read all groups' grants - on the assumption that producers and consumers are cooperative internal services and that topic-level segregation is a convenience rather than a security boundary.
violation: An attacker who compromises any one consumer workload (or its shared service credential) attaches a consumer to topics far outside that service's purpose and silently tees the firehose of business events - PII, transactional records, internal audit and security events flowing through the same bus - because the broker authorizes the principal for the cluster/wildcard rather than for the specific topic this service legitimately consumes, turning the central nervous system of the architecture into a single-credential collection point that produces no application-visible anomaly.

techniques.name: Cross-topic subscription via wildcard or shared-principal consumer ACL
Passive tee of event-stream firehose for bulk collection

rationale: Event buses are the backbone of modern data architectures and carry nearly every category of business data in one place, yet ACL practice defaults to coarse grants - Kafka's default consumer-group ACL is the wildcard '*', shared service principals are common, and managed buses often gate at the cluster or project rather than per-topic - so any single consumer foothold can subscribe broadly. The unmodeled seam is identity-to-authority at the wrong granularity: the bus trusts a consumer principal to self-restrict to its relevant topics, while authorization is enforced at cluster/wildcard scope, so one compromised consumer reads the entire event firehose. This is distinct from cloud-storage collection and from the lakehouse share gap because the trust object is the streaming broker's topic-level consumer authorization and its assumption of cooperative least-privilege consumption, and because the data is in motion - a passive tee leaves no object-access trail and no application error. Per-topic least-privilege enforcement is rarely audited, and detection that a known consumer has begun reading topics outside its historical pattern is essentially absent across the major buses.
suggested_research: Build a bus-authorization analyzer that resolves effective per-principal topic reachability across Kafka/Pub-Sub/Kinesis/EventBridge and flags wildcard or shared-principal over-grants; demonstrate firehose collection from a single compromised consumer, and prototype broker-side detection that baselines per-consumer topic sets and alarms on subscription to topics outside the service's historical footprint.

trust_assumption: An ML feature store guarantees offline/online parity precisely so the online serving layer trusts that a feature value materialized from the offline store is the same vetted value the model trained on, so the online path ingests materialized features as already-validated, provenance-clean inputs and applies no independent integrity or anomaly check at serving time - the parity guarantee is treated as proof of trustworthiness, not merely of equality.
violation: An attacker who can write to the offline feature pipeline (a poisoned upstream source table, a tampered batch transform, or a compromised feature-definition job) injects manipulated feature values that the materialization process then faithfully promotes into the online store, so the poison inherits the parity guarantee's trust and is served to production models in real time - steering high-stakes decisions (fraud scoring, eligibility, agent actions) - while every consistency check passes because offline and online agree exactly on the attacker's value.

techniques.name: Offline feature poisoning promoted to online serving via materialization
Feature-definition/transform job tampering to bias served features

rationale: Feature stores (Feast, Tecton, Databricks Feature Store) exist to enforce offline/online parity, and 2025-2026 practice treats that parity as the integrity guarantee for serving inputs - the online layer trusts materialized features without re-validating provenance because the store promises they match training. The unmodeled seam is context-inheritance: the trust earned by a feature in the offline/training context is inherited wholesale into the online serving context through materialization, so a value poisoned upstream is faithfully promoted and served while parity checks - the only checks present - confirm the poison rather than catch it. This is distinct from classic training-data poisoning (which corrupts a model's weights at train time) because here the live online inference path is steered through the materialization promotion gate, and distinct from RAG/embedding poisoning because the trust object is the feature-store parity contract treated as a provenance guarantee. There is no provenance attestation on materialized features and no serving-time anomaly detection that distinguishes a legitimate feature shift from injected poison that satisfies parity.
suggested_research: Demonstrate end-to-end poisoning that enters an offline feature source, survives a materialization job, and biases a live online prediction while all parity checks pass; then prototype feature-provenance attestation carried through materialization and serving-time anomaly detection that scores feature distributions against attested baselines rather than against the (equally-poisoned) offline copy.

trust_assumption: An MLOps model registry treats a model's promotion to a 'production' stage/alias as an authoritative attestation that this exact artifact passed evaluation and approval, so the serving infrastructure resolves 'the production model' by stage label and loads whatever bytes the registry currently points that label at, trusting that the promoted artifact is byte-identical to the one that was evaluated and that the label binds immutably to that evaluation.
violation: An attacker who can write to the registry or its backing artifact store - a compromised registry credential, a mutable stage alias, or unsigned artifact storage - rebinds the production label to a substituted or backdoored model after evaluation, or swaps the artifact bytes underneath an unchanged label, so the serving layer loads an unevaluated artifact under the trust of a promotion gate it never re-checks; because consumers resolve by label rather than by a signed digest of the evaluated artifact, the malicious model is served as 'the approved production model' with no re-evaluation.

techniques.name: Production-stage alias rebinding to a substituted model artifact
Post-evaluation artifact-byte swap under an unchanged registry label

rationale: Model registries (MLflow, SageMaker, Vertex, Databricks Unity Catalog models) gate production via mutable stage/alias labels, and the dominant 2025-2027 serving pattern resolves the production model by label at deploy/load time, trusting the label as proof the artifact was evaluated and approved. The unmodeled seam is provenance: the promotion event is trusted as an immutable attestation binding label to evaluated bytes, but labels are mutable pointers and backing artifact stores are often unsigned, so the binding between 'evaluated' and 'served' can be broken after the gate without re-evaluation. This is distinct from model-hub deserialization-trust gaps (malicious load-time code) and from leaderboard/eval-overfit gaps because the trust object is specifically the registry promotion gate as a binding attestation - the attacker need not subvert evaluation, only rebind the label or swap bytes afterward. There is little use of signed-digest pinning between evaluation and serving, and detection that a served model's digest diverges from the evaluated artifact is essentially absent.
suggested_research: Demonstrate serving a backdoored model by rebinding a production alias or swapping artifact bytes post-evaluation in a popular registry while the promotion record looks clean; propose signed-digest pinning that binds the production label to the exact evaluated artifact and a serving-time admission check that refuses any model whose digest does not match its evaluation attestation.

trust_assumption: A data catalog / lineage system (Unity Catalog, Purview, OpenLineage, DataHub) is trusted by governance, compliance, and downstream automation as a faithful record of where each dataset came from and how it was derived, so classification labels, quality/certification badges, PII tags, and lineage edges are consumed as authoritative provenance - access policies, audit attestations, and trust-this-table decisions are made from the catalog's claims rather than from the data plane itself.
violation: An attacker who can emit lineage events or edit catalog metadata (an unauthenticated/over-permissive OpenLineage ingest endpoint, a compromised cataloging agent, or write access to tags) forges lineage and certification - laundering a poisoned or unsanctioned dataset to appear derived from a trusted certified source, or stripping PII/sensitivity tags so a restricted table looks public - so downstream consumers, policy engines, and auditors trust attacker-authored provenance metadata that the data plane never substantiates, decoupling the recorded story of the data from its actual origin.

techniques.name: Forged lineage edges laundering dataset provenance
Catalog classification/PII-tag stripping to bypass data policy

rationale: Data catalogs and lineage graphs are increasingly the control plane for data governance in 2025-2027 - access policies, certification trust, and compliance attestations are driven off catalog metadata and emitted lineage (OpenLineage events, scanner-populated tags) - yet that metadata is largely an unauthenticated, easily-emitted side channel decoupled from the data plane it describes. The unmodeled seam is provenance forgery: the catalog's lineage and labels are trusted as ground truth about data origin and sensitivity, but they are attacker-writable assertions, so forged lineage can launder a poisoned dataset as certified-derived and tag-stripping can demote a sensitive table out of policy scope. This is distinct from SLSA build-provenance gaps (artifact build attestation) and from feature-store poisoning because the trust object is the data-catalog's provenance/classification record consumed by governance and humans, not the data or model itself. There is little cryptographic binding between lineage claims and actual data-plane operations, and detection of forged or tampered lineage that contradicts observed reads/writes is absent.
suggested_research: Probe lineage ingest endpoints (OpenLineage, catalog APIs) for unauthenticated or over-permissive event emission and tag mutation; demonstrate laundering an unsanctioned dataset to certified-derived status and stripping PII classification to bypass a policy engine, then propose data-plane-attested lineage that reconciles emitted lineage against observed reads/writes and signs classification changes.

trust_assumption: Disaster-recovery doctrine trusts a backup/snapshot as a clean, point-in-time copy of a known-good state, so the restore path treats snapshot contents as inherently trustworthy ground truth - immutability/object-lock guarantees that the bytes were not altered after write are conflated with a guarantee that the bytes were benign when written, and recovery reintroduces snapshot contents into production with little or no re-scanning of what is actually inside the protected copy.
violation: An attacker with pre-encryption dwell time writes a dormant payload - a backdoor, a logic bomb, or staged credentials - into the production state during the backup window, so it is captured into a snapshot that immutability then faithfully and unalterably preserves; when the victim later restores after an incident, the recovery process reintroduces the attacker's pre-staged implant under the full trust of 'restored from clean backup,' so immutability protects the attacker's payload as diligently as the legitimate data and the restore re-arms the compromise.

techniques.name: Dormant payload pre-staged into protected backup during dwell time
Restore reintroducing implant under clean-backup trust

rationale: Immutable and air-gapped backups are the headline 2025-2026 ransomware defense, with 89% of orgs reporting backup repositories targeted, but the entire control protects against post-write tampering, not pre-write malice - and immutability means a payload captured into a snapshot is preserved as faithfully as the real data. The unmodeled seam is provenance: the restore path trusts a snapshot's integrity (bytes unchanged) as if it were authenticity (bytes were benign at capture), so an attacker who pre-stages a dormant implant before the backup window has it locked into the protected copy and re-armed on recovery. This is distinct from generic backup-destruction tactics (which attack availability) and from the keylog-export archival gap because the trust object is the snapshot's assumed cleanliness as a recovery source, weaponizing immutability itself to guarantee the implant's survival across the very event meant to evict the attacker. Pre-restore deep content scanning of immutable copies and detection of dormant payloads inside backups are nascent, and most recovery runbooks restore-then-scan rather than scan-then-restore.
suggested_research: Model the dwell-window-to-snapshot capture of a dormant implant and demonstrate that immutability preserves it and restore re-arms it; build a pre-restore introspection layer that mounts and deep-scans immutable snapshots for implants/logic bombs before reintroduction, and quantify how long pre-staged payloads survive across backup-retention and restore cycles.

trust_assumption: A managed notebook environment (Databricks/SageMaker/Vertex notebooks, Jupyter/Colab) trusts that any code executed in a kernel is the authoring data scientist's intent, so it gives the kernel ambient inheritance of the user's full data-plane authority - attached workspace credentials, instance/role profiles, mounted lakehouse and warehouse connections, and cached cloud tokens - and treats the running kernel as the user, with no boundary between notebook content and the standing credentials the kernel can reach.
violation: An attacker delivers code into a kernel - a shared/imported notebook, a malicious pip dependency, an %autoreload-triggered module, or an LLM-suggested cell - and that code, running with the kernel's inherited ambient authority, enumerates and exfiltrates the attached credentials and reaches every data system the kernel can touch, because the environment never distinguishes 'the human chose to run this' from 'code in the kernel ran'; the notebook's standing access to lakehouse, warehouse, and secret scopes becomes the blast radius of any code execution, with actions attributed to the data scientist.

techniques.name: Credential and token harvest from inherited notebook kernel context
Malicious imported notebook or dependency executing under kernel authority

rationale: Managed notebooks are where data scientists hold the widest standing access to the data estate in 2025-2027 - kernels run with attached instance roles, workspace credentials, mounted warehouse/lakehouse connections, and secret scopes - and the kernel is trusted as the user with no separation between notebook code and those ambient credentials. The unmodeled seam is context-inheritance: any code reaching the kernel (shared notebook, poisoned dependency, autoreloaded module, or AI-suggested cell) inherits the human's full data-plane authority, so a single code-execution event in a high-access notebook is equivalent to handing over the user's entire data reach. This is distinct from CI build-secret sprawl (durable byproducts of an ephemeral job) and from the citizen-dev connector-borrow gap (low-code shared connections) because the trust object is the interactive notebook kernel's ambient inheritance of a high-privilege human's live data credentials, weaponized by arbitrary in-kernel code and now amplified by AI cell-completion that authors code the human merely accepts. Detection that distinguishes notebook code acting on credentials from the human's interactive intent is absent, and kernel-level credential isolation is immature.
suggested_research: Demonstrate credential harvest and cross-system reach from a single malicious cell or imported notebook in a managed environment with attached roles/secret scopes, including via an AI-suggested cell; prototype kernel-side credential isolation that brokers data-plane access per-action with human-presence confirmation, and detection that flags in-kernel credential enumeration distinct from interactive query patterns.
